Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession numbers section lists BCCP1 as AT5G16390.

Source_Species.reason: The paper studies Arabidopsis and explicitly names Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Title and text identify the species as Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species.reason: Experiments were performed in Arabidopsis seedlings.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species in the experiments is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Main BCCP1 analyses used wild-type Col-0, cdh3, and rescue lines; Col-0 is explicitly stated.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The accession number and main experiments are in Arabidopsis Col-0 background.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results state BCCP1 is a biotin carboxyl carrier protein 1 subunit of chloroplastic heterodimeric ACCase.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper explicitly refers to BCCP1 as part of chloroplastic heterodimeric ACCase.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Figure S4/qRT-PCR text says BCCP1 expression showed no significant transcriptional alteration upon cadaverine treatment.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Western blots analyzed roots and shoots; biotinylated BCCP1 decreased in both, with a larger decrease in roots.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Seedlings were grown on media with 200 µM cadaverine in the BCCP1 analyses.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper links reduced BCCP1 biotinylation to decreased fatty acid biosynthesis and reports reduced TAG abundance after cadaverine treatment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: TAG is a lipid/fatty-acid-related metabolite class.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: TAG is measured and generally is a storage lipid, but this functional phrasing about carbon and energy reserves is not explicitly stated in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Lipidomics was performed on whole seedlings; the text states whole seedlings were harvested for lipid extraction.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: BCCP1 is explicitly described as a subunit of the acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACCase) complex.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show cadaverine decreases BCCP1 biotinylation without significant change in BCCP1 protein abundance, and lipidomics shows decreased TAG accumulation.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All listed points are consistent with Results: BCCP1 biotinylation decreased in roots/shoots, total protein and transcript were not significantly altered, and TAG decreased.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods are all explicitly used: streptavidin blot, BCCP1 antibody blot, qRT-PCR, and tandem MS lipid profiling.

Omics_Data.reason: Lipidomics by tandem mass spectrometry is explicitly described; no accession for lipidomics is given.

Applied_Promoters.reason: No promoter manipulation or promoter-specific BCCP1 construct is described; 'Endogenous promoter; NA' is not stated for BCCP1.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is speculative application language not directly supported by the Results.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper supports that reduced BCCP1 biotinylation is associated with reduced ACCase-related fatty acid biosynthesis and lower TAG content.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper treats BCCP1 as a downstream biotinylated target/effector rather than the primary cadaverine target, which is BIO3-BIO1.

corrections.reason: No promoter manipulation or promoter-specific BCCP1 construct is described; 'Endogenous promoter; NA' is not stated for BCCP1.

corrections.old_value: Useful biomarker of biotin status and fatty-acid synthesis capacity under cadaverine stress; may guide engineering of robust lipid production
corrections.reason: This is speculative application language not directly supported by the Results.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: MCCA is explicitly referred to as 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase and a biotinylated protein involved in amino acid metabolism.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results state MCCA biotinylation changed little overall, while total MCCA protein increased in cdh3 roots upon cadaverine treatment.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: MCCA analyses were performed under cadaverine treatment at 200 µM.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The paper explicitly links MCCA to amino acid metabolism, matching this class-level description.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Discussion states MCCase/MCCA allows recycling of amino acids and is involved in amino acid homeostasis/central metabolism.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: This matches the Results: little overall effect on biotinylated MCCA, but increased MCCA protein in cdh3 roots without corresponding increase in biotinylated MCCA.

corrections.reason: No promoter information or promoter construct for MCCA is described.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession numbers section lists BIO3-BIO1 as AT5G57590.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicit in the title and text.

Applied_Species.reason: Experiments were performed in Arabidopsis seedlings.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The cdh3 mutant was isolated in the Col-0 background.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: BIO3-BIO1 analyses are primarily in Col-0/cdh3 derived from Col-0.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results identify BIO3-BIO1 as a bifunctional enzyme in biotin synthesis; BIO1 is an aminotransferase converting KAPA to DAPA, and BIO3 makes DTB. PLP dependence is indicated by discussion of the PLP cofactor-binding site.

Enzyme_Name.reason: BIO3-BIO1 is explicitly described as the bifunctional biotin biosynthesis enzyme.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The gene is directly placed in biotin biosynthesis throughout the paper.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: BIO3-BIO1 is described as part of the core biotin synthesis pathway.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper states BIO1 converts KAPA to DAPA and DAPA is channeled to BIO3 for DTB synthesis.

Primary_Substrate.reason: KAPA is explicitly named as the BIO1 substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: DAPA is the BIO1 product and DTB is the product detected from the BIO3 reaction.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This matches the explicit pathway description in Results and Figure 2/4 text.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: BIO3-BIO1 is directly involved in synthesis of biotin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The Summary and Results link biotin to biotinylation of carboxylases involved in fatty acid and amino acid metabolism and central metabolism.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: BIO3-BIO1 forms essential intermediates directly leading to biotin synthesis.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper strongly supports BIO3-BIO1 as a key cadaverine target/control point, but it does not explicitly call it rate-limiting.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Figure 4b shows no significant change in BIO3-BIO1 transcript with cadaverine treatment.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results state no significant transcript change with cadaverine, and higher expression in rescue lines correlates with cadaverine resistance.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These treatment details all appear: typically 200 µM cadaverine in seedlings, 50 µM in vitro, and supplementation with biotin/DAPA/KAPA.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The cdh3 A662T mutation causes cadaverine-hypersensitive root growth, and increased BIO3-BIO1 dosage in rescue lines increases cadaverine resistance.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each numbered point is directly supported by the Results: mapping of A662T, transgenic rescue/overexpression effect, biotin and DAPA but not KAPA rescue, in vitro inhibition, and no significant transcript/protein response to cadaverine.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches were explicitly used in the paper.

Omics_Data.reason: Whole-genome sequencing of pooled F2 mutants and lipidomics by tandem MS are explicitly reported; no accession is provided.

Applied_Promoters.reason: A wild-type BIO3-BIO1 transgene under control of its native promoter/BIO3-BIO1pro was used for rescue.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper discusses future crop improvement broadly, but the specific claim about engineering increased BIO3-BIO1 dosage or cadaverine-insensitive variants is a reasonable inference rather than direct evidence.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results support that perturbing this pathway under cadaverine reduces BCCP1 biotinylation, lowers TAG accumulation, alters root growth, and cdh3 shows possible cell death in elongation zone.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The A662T substitution near the PLP binding site is explicit, and the paper states attempts to produce recombinant mutant protein were unsuccessful, suggesting possible stability effects.
